---
title: "Singapore Tourism Recovery Poster"
page-layout: full
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    title-block: false
    css: styles/poster.css
---

::: {.poster-canvas}

::: {.poster-hero}
::: {.hero-kicker}
ISSS608 Visual Analytics & Applications | Singapore Management University
:::

# Singapore Tourism Recovery Through Time-Series Visual Analytics

## A unified visual analytics workflow for source-market demand, recovery trajectories, and short-term forecasting

::: {.hero-meta}
**Team Members**  
Xi Zixun | Wang Zhuoran | Jin Qinhao

**Core Data Backbone**  
Country-level monthly visitor arrivals, interpreted with hotel occupancy, stay length, and room revenue
:::
:::

::: {.poster-grid}

::: {.grid-left}

::: {.section-box .section-intro}
### Motivation and Objective

Tourism recovery after the pandemic did not unfold as one smooth rebound. Different source markets returned at different speeds, while hotel utilisation and visitor stay behaviour reacted with their own lag. A single total-arrivals chart hides too much of this variation.

This project reframes the problem as a **three-step visual analytics workflow**:

- **Explore** how demand changed through shock and reopening.
- **Cluster** countries with similar recovery shapes.
- **Forecast** short-term arrivals for priority source markets.

The goal is to give users one coherent environment for explanation, comparison, and planning rather than a collection of disconnected charts.
:::

::: {.section-box .section-method}
### Data Backbone and Analytical Design

#### Shared data contract
- Monthly visitor arrivals by country are the common analytical backbone.
- Hotel occupancy, average stay length, and room revenue are supporting context variables.

#### Why this structure works
- Keeps all modules aligned to one time-series target.
- Preserves business meaning beyond visitor counts.
- Supports both descriptive and forward-looking analysis.

#### Final workflow
1. Time Series Explorer
2. Recovery Trajectory Clustering
3. Forecasting Source-Market Demand
:::

::: {.section-box .section-app}
### Shiny App Delivery

The final system combines a Quarto project website with a modular Shiny app. Users move through the project using the same logic as the poster:

- inspect the trend and seasonal structure,
- compare grouped recovery patterns,
- run forecast benchmarks on a selected country series.

This keeps the analytical story consistent across documentation, prototype, and app delivery.
:::

:::

::: {.grid-center}

::: {.section-box .section-results}
### Time Series Explorer

The explorer establishes the main narrative: a severe demand collapse in 2020, an uneven reopening period, and a recovery that differs across source markets. It also links arrivals back to tourism performance.

#### Result highlights
- China and total arrivals collapse together in the shock period.
- The rebound is real but not perfectly symmetric with the pre-pandemic pattern.
- Hotel occupancy still rises with returning demand, showing that arrivals remain operationally meaningful.

::: {.figure-frame}
![](/C:/Users/12697/Desktop/VAA%20final/poster-assets/eda-china-market.png)
*Source-market demand and total arrivals do not recover in one identical path.*
:::

::: {.figure-frame}
![](/C:/Users/12697/Desktop/VAA%20final/poster-assets/eda-hotel-utilisation.png)
*The explorer links market recovery back to accommodation performance.*
:::
:::

::: {.section-box .section-cluster}
### Clustering Recovery Trajectories

Clustering shifts the story from "what happened" to "which markets behave similarly." The clustering unit is the **country trajectory**, which keeps the method aligned to the shared arrivals backbone.

#### Result highlights
- Recovery shapes naturally separate into interpretable groups.
- The time path reveals a persistent shift from shock to recovery, not random monthly noise.
- A compact cluster solution is easier to explain and expose inside the Shiny app.

::: {.two-up}
::: {.figure-frame}
![](/C:/Users/12697/Desktop/VAA%20final/poster-assets/cluster-scatter.png)
*The reduced-space view separates recovery shapes into meaningful groups.*
:::

::: {.figure-frame}
![](/C:/Users/12697/Desktop/VAA%20final/poster-assets/cluster-timeline.png)
*Cluster chronology shows how the tourism system moved through recovery phases.*
:::
:::

::: {.figure-frame .figure-compact}
![](/C:/Users/12697/Desktop/VAA%20final/poster-assets/cluster-elbow.png)
*The elbow diagnostic supports a compact and explainable clustering solution.*
:::
:::

:::

::: {.grid-right}

::: {.section-box .section-forecast}
### Forecasting Source-Market Demand

The forecasting module follows the Chapter 19 and 20 workflow from *R for Visual Analytics*: inspect the series, confirm seasonal structure, build a time-aware split, benchmark models, and compare their holdout accuracy.

#### Result highlights
- The selected source market keeps clear seasonal structure even after the shock period.
- Decomposition reveals the trend break and seasonal pattern before forecasting begins.
- Seasonal Naive, ETS, and ARIMA are evaluated on one shared holdout horizon.

::: {.figure-frame}
![](/C:/Users/12697/Desktop/VAA%20final/poster-assets/forecast-series.png)
*The forecasting input preserves shock, reopening, and renewed seasonal peaks.*
:::

::: {.figure-frame}
![](/C:/Users/12697/Desktop/VAA%20final/poster-assets/forecast-decomposition.png)
*Decomposition separates long-run movement, seasonality, and residual noise.*
:::

::: {.figure-frame}
![](/C:/Users/12697/Desktop/VAA%20final/poster-assets/forecast-holdout.png)
*A shared holdout window makes model comparison transparent and defensible.*
:::
:::

::: {.section-box .section-summary}
### Main Findings

- Tourism recovery is better understood as a **time-series system** than as one aggregate total.
- Country-level arrivals provide a shared backbone that keeps exploration, clustering, and forecasting consistent.
- Hotel occupancy and stay indicators give the demand signal business meaning.
- The final product supports descriptive analysis and short-term planning in the same interface.
:::

::: {.section-box .section-future}
### Future Work

- Extend forecasting to more source markets and transport modes.
- Deploy the Shiny app to a public host for broader use.
- Turn cluster outputs into reusable recovery personas for planning and policy communication.
:::

:::

:::

::: {.poster-footer}
::: {.footer-box}
**Approach**  
Time-series exploration | clustering of recovery trajectories | forecasting benchmark comparison
:::

::: {.footer-box}
**Packages and Delivery**  
Quarto, Shiny, tidyverse, plotly, tsibble, fable, timetk, modeltime
:::

::: {.footer-box}
**Poster Narrative**  
Explore demand -> identify recovery patterns -> support forward planning
:::
:::

:::
